summary: I was born in East Los Angeles on July 25, 1912, in a section then called Belvedere. Growing up, East Los Angeles was a developing area just east of the city limits of Los Angeles, which stopped at Indiana Street. It was a place where my family lived and where I attended schools like Garfield High School after junior high. East Los Angeles has always been closely tied to my early life and education.

Belvedere was the name of the area where I was born and raised, just east of Indiana Street and outside the city limits of Los Angeles. It was an unincorporated area in Los Angeles County before it became part of what is now called East Los Angeles. I lived at 225 South Herbert Street in Belvedere and attended Belvedere Elementary and Junior High Schools there. Belvedere was a close-knit community during my childhood.

Belvedere was part of Los Angeles County, just adjacent to the city of Los Angeles. The county area included places like the Los Angeles Creamery Company lands and Calvary Cemetery, which marked boundaries near where I lived. My early life and schooling took place within this county jurisdiction before the area was more fully developed. Los Angeles County was the larger region encompassing my hometown.

Los Angeles was the city just west of where I grew up, with its boundary stopping at Indiana Street during my childhood. It was the larger urban center near my home in Belvedere and East Los Angeles. I often referenced Los Angeles in relation to my neighborhood and schooling, as well as the development of areas around it. The city was a significant point of reference for my family's life and work.

Indiana Street was the eastern boundary of the city of Los Angeles when I was growing up. My neighborhood of Belvedere was just east of Indiana Street, in the unincorporated area of Los Angeles County. This street marked the division between the city and county lands, including places like Calvary Cemetery and the Los Angeles Creamery Company grounds. Indiana Street was a key geographic marker in my early life.

The Janss Investment Company was a major developer responsible for subdividing Belvedere Gardens and later developing Westwood, Los Angeles. They also donated large acreage to the University of California, Los Angeles. Janss played a significant role in shaping the neighborhoods around where I grew up, including Burbank, which they may have developed earlier. Their work influenced the growth of the areas I knew well as a child.

Belvedere Gardens was a subdivision developed by the Janss Investment Company after my childhood in the original Belvedere area. It became part of what is now known as East Los Angeles. While I lived in the older Belvedere, Belvedere Gardens represented the expansion and development of the community in later years. It was a neighboring area that grew alongside my own neighborhood.

Westwood was developed by the Janss Investment Company after their work in Belvedere and Belvedere Gardens. It is notable because the Janss company donated large tracts of land there to establish UCLA. Although I did not live in Westwood, its development was connected to the same company that influenced my home area. Westwood represents the broader growth of Los Angeles neighborhoods in the early 20th century.

UCLA was established on land donated by the Janss Investment Company, which also developed areas near where I grew up. While I did not attend UCLA myself, the university's founding was closely tied to the development of Westwood, a neighborhood created by Janss. UCLA stands as an important educational institution in Los Angeles, connected to the growth of the region I know well.

Burbank was another area that the Janss Investment Company was responsible for subdividing, possibly even before their work in Belvedere. Though I did not live in Burbank, it was part of the broader development of Los Angeles County that shaped the region. Burbank's growth was linked to the same forces that influenced my neighborhood and family life.

Calvary Cemetery was a landmark near where I grew up, marking the boundary at Eastern Avenue just east of the Los Angeles Creamery Company lands. It served as a geographic reference point in the Belvedere area of Los Angeles County. The cemetery was part of the landscape surrounding my childhood neighborhood and helped define the limits of the developed areas at the time.

My mother, Martha Bohmke, came to the United States from Germany when she was sixteen years old. She arrived not knowing the language and stayed with her older sister in Chicago before eventually settling in California. She was entirely a homemaker and placed great importance on learning a trade, which influenced me when I found my interest in printing. Her background and values shaped much of my upbringing.

Germany was the country my mother, Martha Bohmke, emigrated from when she was sixteen. She left Germany without knowing English and came to America to live with her sister in Chicago. Her German heritage was an important part of my family history and influenced the values she passed on to me. This connection to Germany shaped the immigrant experience in my family.

Chicago was the city where my mother stayed when she first came to the United States from Germany. She lived with her older sister there before moving west to California. Chicago was the starting point of her American journey and a place that connected her old world to the new. It was part of the family story that led to my birth and upbringing in California.

My father was originally from Pennsylvania, and he came to California as a boy with his uncle. His family, including his father who was a doctor, lived in Stockton, California, but their roots were in Pennsylvania. This background gave me a sense of the family's migration westward and the blend of East Coast and West Coast influences in my life. Pennsylvania was part of my father's heritage.

Stockton was where my father's family lived after moving from Pennsylvania. His father was a doctor there, and it was part of the family's early California history. Although I was born and raised in East Los Angeles, Stockton represents the earlier chapter of my father's side of the family. It connects to the story of how my family settled in California.

Robert was my oldest brother, but sadly he died of the flu when he was only nine years old. He was the firstborn in our family, preceding Theodore, myself, and our younger brother Carl. His early death was a significant loss for our family during my childhood. I remember him as part of my family history.

My second oldest brother, Theodore, was named after President Theodore Roosevelt, who was in office when he was born. This naming reflected the admiration my family had for the president at the time. Theodore was part of my immediate family, growing up alongside me and our brothers Robert and Carl. The connection to Roosevelt gave a historical context to our family names.

Theodore was my second oldest brother, named after President Theodore Roosevelt. He was born during Roosevelt's presidency, which inspired his name. We grew up together in East Los Angeles, along with our brothers Robert and Carl. Theodore was part of the close family circle of my childhood.

Carl was my youngest brother, born after me and my older brothers Robert and Theodore. We all grew up together in Belvedere, East Los Angeles. Carl was part of the family dynamic that shaped my early years. Our siblings shared the experiences of growing up in that community.

I attended Belvedere Elementary School from first through eighth grade, located at the corner of First and Rowan avenues. Charles J. Fox was the principal during my time there. This school was where I received my foundational education growing up in Belvedere. It was an important part of my early life and community.

Charles J. Fox was the principal of Belvedere Elementary School when I attended from first through eighth grade. After that, he became the principal of the new Belvedere Junior High School, where I was president of the student body in ninth grade. He was a consistent figure in my schooling during those years. His leadership influenced the schools I attended in Belvedere.

I attended Belvedere Junior High School for only one year, the ninth grade, right after graduating from Belvedere Elementary. The school had just opened, and Charles J. Fox was the principal there as well. During that year, I was president of the student body and became very interested in the junior high printshop. It was a formative experience that led me toward my future career in printing.

Chester L. Bond was my printing teacher at Belvedere Junior High School. He was a Seventh-day Adventist who had worked at the Adventist publishing plant in Mountain View near Palo Alto. His interest and teaching inspired me to become firmly attached to printing. He was an excellent printer and a significant influence during my early vocational education.

Chester L. Bond, my printing teacher, was a member of the Seventh-day Adventist Church. He had worked at the Adventist publishing plant in Mountain View, near Palo Alto. This religious affiliation was part of his background and influenced his work in printing. While I was not a member, the church was connected to my early vocational training through him.

Mountain View was the location of the Seventh-day Adventist publishing plant where my printing teacher, Chester L. Bond, had worked before teaching me. It is near Palo Alto and was known for its Adventist printing operations. Though I never lived there, Mountain View was part of the background of my vocational education in printing. It connected to the religious and professional training I received.

Palo Alto is near Mountain View, where the Seventh-day Adventist publishing plant was located. My printing teacher, Chester L. Bond, worked there before coming to teach at Belvedere Junior High. While I did not live in Palo Alto, it was part of the regional context for my early printing education. The area was significant in the Adventist printing community connected to my schooling.

After finishing Belvedere Junior High School, I went on to attend Garfield High School in East Los Angeles, which had just opened at the time. It was an interesting experience to be part of the first group of students there. Garfield High School continued my education within the East Los Angeles community where I was raised. It marked the next stage of my schooling after junior high.
